days.title: Week 2, Day 1: Asking for Help

words.example_sentence_native: Excuse me, where is the train station?
Excuse me, do you speak English?

words.example_sentence_target: Es tut mir leid, ich verstehe nicht.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Es toot meer lite, ish fer-shtay-uh nisht.

sentences.native: Where can I find a toilet?

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Excuse me, can you help me?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Yes, of course. What are you looking for?

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Der Bus fährt um drei Uhr ab.
lines.native: The bus leaves at three o'clock.
lines.phonetic: Der boos fairt oom dry oor ap.

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Entschuldigung, ich verstehe nicht. Können Sie das wiederholen?
lines.native: Sorry, I don't understand. Can you repeat that?
lines.phonetic: Ent-shool-di-goong, ish fer-shtay-uh nisht. Kurn-en zee das vee-der-hoh-len?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Ein wenig. Wie kann ich Ihnen helfen?
lines.native: A little. How can I help you?
lines.phonetic: Eyen vay-nish. Vee kan ish ee-nen hel-fen?

days.cultural_note: Germans are generally direct and appreciate politeness. Starting a request with 'Entschuldigung' (Excuse me) is always a good idea.
days.pronunciation_tip: The German 'ch' sound in 'Ich' is soft, made in the front of the mouth. It's different from the harder 'ch' in 'Bach' which comes from the back of the throat.

days.title: Week 2, Day 2: Understanding Questions

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: How do I get to the airport?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Nehmen Sie die U-Bahn, Linie U6.
lines.phonetic: Nay-men zee dee oo-bahn, lee-nee-uh oo-zeks.

days.cultural_note: German question words (W-Fragen) like Wer, Was, Wann, Wo, Warum, Wie usually come at the beginning of the sentence.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'W' in German words like 'Wo' and 'Was' is pronounced like the English 'V'.

days.title: Week 2, Day 3: Giving Simple Information

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Ist das der Zug nach Berlin?
lines.native: Is this the train to Berlin?
lines.phonetic: Ist das der tsook nakh Ber-leen?

fillBlanks.hint: Yes, I speak a little German.

days.cultural_note: When giving your name, you can say 'Ich bin [Name]' (I am [Name]) or 'Mein Name ist [Name]' (My name is [Name]). Both are common.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'ei' in 'Nein' is pronounced like the 'i' in the English word 'nine'.

days.title: Week 2, Day 4: Asking for Location
days.title_native: Woche 2, Tag 4: Nach dem Ort fragen

words.example_sentence_native: What is the name of this street?

words.example_sentence_target: Gibt es hier ein Café in der Nähe?
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Gipt es heer eyn ka-fay in der nay-uh?

words.example_sentence_target: Kann ich das auf der Karte sehen?
words.example_sentence_native: Can I see that on the map?
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Kan ish das owf der kar-tuh zay-en?

sentences.native: I am looking for the post office.

sentences.target: Das Hotel ist in der Schillerstraße.
sentences.native: The hotel is on Schiller Street.
sentences.phonetic: Das ho-tel ist in der Shil-ler-shtrah-suh.

sentences.target: Zeigen Sie es mir auf der Karte.
sentences.native: Show it to me on the map.
sentences.phonetic: Tseyegen zee es meer owf der kar-tuh.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Excuse me, where is Main Street?

dialogues.context: Asking if a place is far.

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Nein, es sind nur fünf Minuten zu Fuß.
lines.native: No, it's only a five-minute walk.
lines.phonetic: Nine, es zint noor fewnf mi-noo-ten tsoo foos.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Was ist die Adresse vom Restaurant?
lines.phonetic: Vas ist dee a-dres-uh fom res-to-rong?

dialogues.context: Finding a place on a map.

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Hier, ich zeige es Ihnen auf der Karte.
lines.native: Here, I'll show you on the map.
lines.phonetic: Heer, ish tseye-guh es ee-nen owf der kar-tuh.

days.cultural_note: In Germany, street names often come after the name (e.g., 'Goethestraße'). The number follows the street name.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'ß' (called 'Eszett') is pronounced like a sharp 's' sound. It's found in words like 'Straße' (street).

days.title: Week 2, Day 5: Directions

words.example_sentence_target: Gehen Sie an der Ampel links.
words.example_sentence_native: Turn left at the traffic light.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Gay-en zee an der am-pel links.

words.example_sentence_target: Die Bäckerei ist auf der rechten Seite.
words.example_sentence_native: The bakery is on the right side.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Dee bek-uh-reye ist owf der resh-ten zeye-tuh.

words.example_sentence_target: Die Apotheke ist an der Ecke.
words.example_sentence_native: The pharmacy is at the corner.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Dee apo-tay-kuh ist an der ek-uh.

words.example_sentence_target: Der Park ist über die Straße.
words.example_sentence_native: The park is across the street.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Der park ist ew-ber dee shtrah-suh.

words.example_sentence_target: Die Bank ist neben der Post.
words.example_sentence_native: The bank is next to the post office.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Dee bank ist nay-ben der post.

sentences.native: The first street on the left.
The second street on the right.

sentences.target: Es ist auf der linken Seite.
sentences.phonetic: Es ist owf der lin-ken zeye-tuh.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Excuse me, where is the pharmacy?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Gehen Sie geradeaus und dann die erste Straße rechts.
lines.native: Go straight and then the first street on the right.
lines.phonetic: Gay-en zee ge-rah-duh-ows oont dan dee air-stuh shtrah-suh reshts.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Ist das Restaurant an der Ecke?
lines.native: Is the restaurant on the corner?
lines.phonetic: Ist das res-to-rong an der ek-uh?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Yes, exactly. Next to the cinema.

dialogues.context: Asking for directions to the subway.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: How do I get to the subway?

dialogues.context: Being told you're going the wrong way.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Gehen Sie in die andere Richtung.
lines.native: You are going in the other direction.
lines.phonetic: Gay-en zee in dee an-duh-ruh rish-toong.

sentenceScramble.correct: Die Bank ist neben der Post
sentenceScramble.nativeHint: The bank is next to the post office.

days.cultural_note: Germans often use landmarks for directions. It's helpful to know words like 'die Ampel' (traffic light), 'die Kreuzung' (intersection), and 'die Brücke' (bridge).
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'r' at the start of a word like 'rechts' is lightly rolled or gargled in the back of the throat in standard German.

days.title: Week 2, Day 6: Public Places

words.example_sentence_target: Der Zug fährt vom Bahnhof ab.
words.example_sentence_native: The train departs from the station.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Der tsook fairt fom barn-hohf ap.

words.example_sentence_native: We have to go to the airport.
I have to go to the hospital.

words.example_sentence_target: Ich gehe zur Bank, um Geld abzuheben.
words.example_sentence_native: I'm going to the bank to withdraw money.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Ish gay-uh tsoor bank, oom gelt ap-tsoo-hay-ben.

words.example_sentence_target: Lass uns in ein Restaurant gehen.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Las oons in eyn res-to-rong gay-en.

sentences.native: I have to go to the train station.

sentences.target: Gibt es hier eine Bank in der Nähe?
sentences.phonetic: Gipt es heer eyen-uh bank in der nay-uh?

sentences.target: Der Flughafen ist nicht weit von hier.
sentences.native: The airport is not far from here.
sentences.phonetic: Der flook-hah-fen ist nisht veyt fon heer.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Excuse me, I'm looking for the hospital.

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Es ist zwei Straßen weiter, auf der rechten Seite.
lines.native: It's two streets further, on the right side.
lines.phonetic: Es ist tsveye shtrah-sen veye-ter, owf der resh-ten zeye-tuh.

dialogues.context: In a taxi to the airport.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Können Sie ein gutes Restaurant empfehlen?
lines.native: Can you recommend a good restaurant?
lines.phonetic: Kurn-en zee eyn goo-tes res-to-rong em-fay-len?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Ja, das Restaurant 'Zum Bären' ist sehr gut.
lines.native: Yes, the restaurant 'To the Bear' is very good.
lines.phonetic: Ya, das res-to-rong 'Tsoom Bay-ren' ist zair goot.

fillBlanks.sentence: Der Zug nach München fährt von ___ 5 ab.
fillBlanks.hint: The train to Munich leaves from station 5.

days.cultural_note: In Germany, public services like hospitals and police are very reliable. The emergency number for police is 110 and for an ambulance/fire department is 112.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'h' in 'Bahnhof' is silent; it lengthens the 'a' vowel sound before it. So it sounds like 'Barn-hohf'.

days.title: Week 2, Day 7: Travel

words.example_sentence_native: When does the next train leave?

words.example_sentence_target: Können Sie mir ein Taxi rufen?
words.example_sentence_native: Can you call me a taxi?
words.phonetic: Kurn-en zee meer eyn tak-see roo-fen?

words.example_sentence_target: Ich brauche eine Fahrkarte nach Berlin.
words.example_sentence_native: I need a ticket to Berlin.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Ish brow-khuh eyen-uh far-kar-tuh nakh Ber-leen.
Der tsook fairt fon gleyss dry.

sentences.native: I would like to go to Hamburg.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Von welchem Gleis fährt der Zug?
lines.native: From which platform does the train depart?
lines.phonetic: Fon vel-shem gleyss fairt der tsook?

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Does this bus go to the city center?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Nein, Sie müssen den Bus Nummer 5 nehmen.
lines.native: No, you have to take bus number 5.
lines.phonetic: Nine, zee mews-en den boos noom-mer fewnf nay-men.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Ich habe einen Koffer zum Aufgeben.
lines.native: I have one suitcase to check in.
lines.phonetic: Ish ha-buh eyen-en kof-fer tsoom owf-gay-ben.

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Stellen Sie ihn bitte auf die Waage.
lines.native: Please put it on the scale.
lines.phonetic: Shtel-en zee een bit-tuh owf dee vah-guh.

fillBlanks.hint: I need a ticket to Cologne.

days.cultural_note: In Germany, you often need to validate (stamp) your ticket in a small machine before boarding a train, tram, or bus. Forgetting to do this can result in a fine.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'z' in 'Zug' is pronounced like 'ts' in English 'cats'. So, 'Tsook'.
